items.q: What is the dramatic function of a soliloquy?
items.correct: It reveals a character's inner thoughts directly to the audience while the character is alone on stage — a convention granting access to private psychology.
items.misconception: A soliloquy is any long speech a character gives.
items.random_wrong: A soliloquy is when characters argue with each other.
items.why: Students confuse soliloquy (alone, inner thoughts) with monologue (long speech, possibly to others). The key feature is solitude and direct access to thought — losing this loses the convention's force.

items.q: Why must students distinguish the narrator from the author?
items.correct: The narrator is a constructed voice within the text; the author is the real person who created it. Conflating them misreads irony, unreliable narration and persona.
items.misconception: The narrator's views are always the author's views.
items.random_wrong: The narrator and author are different words for the same thing.
items.why: Students attribute everything a first-person narrator says to the author. This collapses irony — a novel like 'Lolita' cannot work if you take Humbert as Nabokov.

items.q: What is the difference between a theme and a symbol?
items.correct: A theme is an abstract idea or concern the text explores (e.g. mortality); a symbol is a concrete image that stands for something else (e.g. a skull).
items.misconception: Themes are just symbols repeated through the book.
items.random_wrong: A theme is the title of the book.
items.why: Students treat theme and symbol as the same thing. Themes are ideas, symbols are images. Skulls (symbol) can develop the theme (mortality), but they are not the same level of abstraction.

items.q: What did Romantic poets value in nature?
items.correct: Nature as a source of spiritual insight and emotional truth, accessed through imagination — not picturesque scenery for its own sake.
items.misconception: Romantic poets just liked to write about pretty landscapes.
items.random_wrong: Romantic poets believed nature was full of fairies and elves.
items.why: The Romantic engagement with nature was philosophical — it was a route to truth, the sublime and the self. Reducing it to 'pretty scenery' misses Wordsworth's epistemology entirely.

items.q: What are conventions of the Gothic genre?
items.correct: Settings of ruin and decay, supernatural or psychological terror, transgression of boundaries, the sublime, doubled or haunted protagonists, and atmospheric excess.
items.misconception: Gothic just means scary stories with vampires and ghosts.
items.random_wrong: Gothic refers to medieval architecture, not literature.
items.why: Students reduce Gothic to monsters. The genre is defined by its concerns — boundaries, the past intruding on the present, the limits of reason — and only sometimes uses supernatural creatures.

items.q: What characterises literary modernism?
items.correct: Experimentation with form (fragmentation, stream of consciousness), focus on subjective interior experience, scepticism toward traditional narrative and absolute truth.
items.misconception: Modernism just means literature written in modern times.
items.random_wrong: Modernism is literature about modern technology like phones.
items.why: Modernism is a specific early 20th-century movement (Joyce, Woolf, Eliot), not a synonym for contemporary. Students use it as a vague chronological term, missing its formal experimentation.

items.q: What is iambic pentameter?
items.correct: A line of five iambs (unstressed-stressed syllable pairs), creating a ten-syllable line with the rhythm da-DUM da-DUM da-DUM da-DUM da-DUM.
items.misconception: Iambic pentameter is any line with ten syllables.
items.random_wrong: Iambic pentameter is when every line rhymes.
items.why: Syllable count alone doesn't make iambic pentameter — the stress pattern is essential. And iambic pentameter is usually unrhymed (blank verse) in Shakespeare's plays.

items.q: What is hamartia in tragedy?
items.correct: A fatal error or flaw in the tragic hero that leads to their downfall — sometimes a character defect, sometimes a mistake in judgement.
items.misconception: Hamartia is the tragic hero's evil personality.
items.random_wrong: Hamartia is the moment the tragic hero dies.
items.why: Tragic heroes are not villains. Hamartia is often a noble quality taken too far (Othello's love, Macbeth's ambition) or a misjudgement — not simple wickedness.

items.q: What is dramatic irony?
items.correct: When the audience knows something a character does not, creating tension between the character's words/actions and the audience's understanding.
items.misconception: Dramatic irony is when something ironic happens in a play.
items.random_wrong: Dramatic irony is when characters speak in dramatic voices.
items.why: Students treat 'dramatic irony' as just irony in a drama. The defining feature is the gap between audience and character knowledge — without that gap, it isn't dramatic irony.

items.q: What is the structure of a Shakespearean sonnet?
items.correct: Fourteen lines in iambic pentameter, structured as three quatrains and a final couplet, rhyming ABAB CDCD EFEF GG, often with a volta near the couplet.
items.misconception: A sonnet is any short love poem.
items.random_wrong: A sonnet must always be exactly ten lines long.
items.why: Students treat sonnet as a vague genre. It is a strict 14-line form with specific rhyme schemes — and sonnets need not be about love (think Hopkins's religious sonnets).

items.q: What is verbal irony?
items.correct: When a speaker says one thing but means another — typically the opposite — relying on shared context for the listener to detect the gap.
items.misconception: Verbal irony is just saying something unexpected.
items.random_wrong: Verbal irony is when you make a joke about something.
items.why: Students conflate irony with surprise or humour. Irony specifically involves a gap between literal and intended meaning — a more precise device than 'unexpected'.

items.q: What is the difference between a metaphor and a simile?
items.correct: A simile compares using 'like' or 'as'; a metaphor states one thing IS another, making an implicit comparison without those connectives.
items.misconception: Metaphors are short and similes are long.
items.random_wrong: Metaphors are true and similes are false.
items.why: Length is irrelevant. The defining difference is the use of 'like'/'as' in similes — metaphors omit them, making the comparison stronger and more direct.

items.q: What makes a narrator unreliable?
items.correct: Their account is undermined within the text by ignorance, bias, self-deception or deceit, so the reader must read against what they say.
items.misconception: An unreliable narrator is one who tells lies in the story.
items.random_wrong: An unreliable narrator is when the author makes mistakes.
items.why: Unreliability is structural — the text gives signals the narrator is wrong (e.g. internal contradictions). Lying intentionally is one kind, but blind spots and self-deception count too.

items.q: What is foreshadowing?
items.correct: Subtle hints or clues earlier in a text that anticipate later events, making the resolution feel both surprising and inevitable.
items.misconception: Foreshadowing is when a character predicts the future.
items.random_wrong: Foreshadowing is the shadow a character casts in a scene.
items.why: Foreshadowing works on the reader, not within the story world. A character's prediction is a plot event — foreshadowing is an authorial technique embedded in detail and tone.

items.q: Why does historical context matter when reading literature?
items.correct: Texts emerge from and respond to specific historical conditions — context illuminates references, assumptions and arguments that might otherwise be invisible.
items.misconception: Context just means writing about what the author had for breakfast.
items.random_wrong: Context is required because all texts are about history.
items.why: Students treat context as biographical trivia. Useful context is what the text engages with — politics, ideology, genre conventions — not personal detail of the author's life.

items.q: What is the structural framework of The Canterbury Tales?
items.correct: A frame narrative — pilgrims travelling to Canterbury tell stories along the way, allowing Chaucer to juxtapose different voices, genres and social classes.
items.misconception: It is one continuous story told by Chaucer from beginning to end.
items.random_wrong: It is a religious text used in Catholic services.
items.why: Students miss the frame structure. Chaucer's strategy is polyphony — voicing many speakers to compare and contrast — not a single narrative voice.

items.q: Why distinguish the speaker of a poem from the poet?
items.correct: The speaker is the voice constructed in the poem; the poet is the writer. Treating them as identical can mislead, especially with dramatic monologues or personae.
items.misconception: The speaker of a poem is always the poet themselves.
items.random_wrong: The speaker is whoever reads the poem out loud.
items.why: Browning's 'My Last Duchess' is not Browning — it is a murderous Duke. Students collapse speaker into poet, losing the persona and the moral complexity that comes with it.

items.q: What is enjambment?
items.correct: When a sentence or phrase runs over from one line of poetry to the next without a punctuated pause, creating flow or surprise across the line break.
items.misconception: Enjambment is when a poem has very long lines.
items.random_wrong: Enjambment is when a poem uses no punctuation at all.
items.why: Enjambment is about syntax crossing the line break, not line length or punctuation in general. Students miss that it is about the relationship between grammar and lineation.

items.q: Why are genre conventions important when interpreting a text?
items.correct: They set reader expectations the text can fulfil or subvert; analysing how a text uses or breaks conventions reveals its meaning and significance.
items.misconception: Genre is just a label to help bookshops categorise novels.
items.random_wrong: Genre means whether a book is fiction or non-fiction.
items.why: Genre is interpretively active. A horror novel that subverts horror conventions does so meaningfully — but only if you know the conventions. Students treat genre as labelling, not as a reading lens.

items.q: What is the 'intentional fallacy'?
items.correct: The claim that judging a text by what the author 'intended' is fallacious — the text's meaning lies in the work itself, not the author's stated or guessed intentions.
items.misconception: It means authors can never make mistakes when writing.
items.random_wrong: It means it is wrong to write fiction.
items.why: Students assume 'author intent' settles meaning. Wimsatt and Beardsley argued it doesn't — meaning is a public property of the text, not a private fact about the author.

items.q: What is the function of dystopian fiction?
items.correct: To critique tendencies in the writer's own society by extrapolating them into a nightmarish future — dystopias hold up a warning mirror to the present.
items.misconception: Dystopian fiction is just storytelling set in scary futures.
items.random_wrong: Dystopian fiction predicts what will actually happen in 100 years.
items.why: Dystopias are about the present, not the future. 1984 critiques 1948; The Handmaid's Tale critiques 1980s America. Students miss the satirical function and read them as prophecy.

items.q: What is a bildungsroman?
items.correct: A novel tracing a protagonist's moral and psychological development from youth to adulthood — typically involving formative experiences and a clearer sense of self.
items.misconception: Any novel with a young protagonist is a bildungsroman.
items.random_wrong: A bildungsroman is a novel about constructing buildings.
items.why: The focus must be on inward growth and development — having a young protagonist isn't enough. Students apply the term loosely to any story featuring a child or teenager.

items.q: What defines epic poetry as a genre?
items.correct: Long narrative poems on heroic subjects, in elevated style, often invoking a muse, in medias res openings, and treating events of national or cosmic significance.
items.misconception: Epic means any poem that is very long.
items.random_wrong: Epic means any story with a battle in it.
items.why: Length alone isn't enough. Epic has specific conventions (invocation, elevated diction, heroic subject) inherited from Homer through Virgil to Milton — students reduce it to scale.

items.q: What is pathetic fallacy?
items.correct: Attributing human emotions or qualities to nature or inanimate objects to reflect a character's feelings or scene's mood (e.g. angry storms during conflict).
items.misconception: Pathetic fallacy is when an argument in a story is weak or sad.
items.random_wrong: Pathetic fallacy is when characters cry over silly things.
items.why: The term uses 'pathetic' in its old sense — pertaining to emotion (pathos) — not 'feeble'. Students misread it as a value judgement when it is a technical device coined by Ruskin.

items.q: What characterises postmodern literature?
items.correct: Self-aware metafiction, intertextuality, scepticism toward grand narratives, playfulness with form, and blurring of high/low culture.
items.misconception: Postmodernism just means literature written after modernism.
items.random_wrong: Postmodernism means writing about the future.
items.why: Postmodernism is a specific set of attitudes and techniques, not a date range. A novel written in 2024 may be entirely traditional and not postmodern in any meaningful sense.
